1: 'It is said: If a man divorces his wife and she goes and marries another, can he take her back? Would not the land be totally defiled?' — Jeremiah applies the divorce-and-remarriage law of Deuteronomy 24:1-4 to the YHWH-Israel relationship. The Deuteronomic law prohibited a first husband from taking back a wife who had remarried after the divorce — doing so would 'defile the land.' Jeremiah's application makes the logic striking: by the standard law, Israel's worship of other gods (marriage to other lovers) has permanently disqualified her from returning to YHWH. The 'yet return to me' at the end of the verse is theologically extraordinary — YHWH is offering what the law explicitly forbids, demonstrating that his covenant grace exceeds the law's terms.
2: 'Raise your eyes to the bare hilltops and look — is there any place you have not been violated?' — the šĕpāyim (bare hills, treeless heights) were the standard Canaanite high-place locations (cf. Jeremiah 2:20: 'every high hill and under every spreading tree'). The violation (šikkabta) imagery combines the sexual/idolatrous register: the high places were sites of cult prostitution as well as Baal sacrifice. 'Sitting by the roads waiting for lovers, like a nomad lurking in the desert' continues the prostitution metaphor — Israel did not merely passively participate in Canaanite cult but actively sought it out.
3: 'Therefore the rain has been withheld, and the spring rain has not come' — the withheld rain is the covenant curse of Deuteronomy 28:23-24 ('the sky above you will be bronze, the earth below you iron; the LORD will turn the rain of your country into dust and powder'). Rain in Palestine was the primary form of agricultural providence — almost entirely rain-fed, the land depended on the early (October-November) and latter (April-May) rains. Baal was specifically the storm and fertility deity; the irony is that worshipping Baal for rain produced drought — YHWH, not Baal, controls the rain (1 Kings 18:1: YHWH ends the drought when Baal worship is confronted at Carmel).
4: 'Have you not just been calling out to me, “My Father, you are my closest companion from my youth”?' — the appeal to divine fatherhood in the midst of ongoing apostasy is called out as hypocrisy. The 'my Father' (ʾābî) invocation acknowledges the fundamental covenant relationship (YHWH as Father: Deuteronomy 32:6; Isaiah 63:16; Jeremiah 64:8) while the behavior contradicts the filial relationship it claims. The tension between the genuine need for divine paternal care and the unwillingness to actually live as a covenant child is the same dynamic Jesus addresses in the 'Lord, Lord' sayings (Matthew 7:21).
5: 'Will he be angry forever? Will he stay angry to the end? But see — you have spoken, and you have done all the evil you could' — the rhetorical question expects the answer 'of course not; his anger will pass' — a presumption on the divine mercy that is then punctured by the second clause. The people use the correct theological data (YHWH's anger does not last forever: Psalms 30:5; Jeremiah 103:9) to excuse continued sin, without acknowledging that the prerequisite for the anger's passing is repentance, not simply the passage of time. Jeremiah punctures the false comfort: 'you have done all the evil you could' — the sin is not isolated behavior but comprehensive maximizing of wickedness.
6: 'The LORD said to me in the days of King Josiah: Have you seen what faithless Israel has done? She has gone up to every high mountain and under every spreading tree and committed adultery there' — the historical flashback to Josiah's reign frames the Israel-Judah comparison. The Northern Kingdom fell to Assyria in 722 BCE under Sargon II, approximately ninety years before Jeremiah's call; Josiah is attempting to reform Judah during the period of Assyrian weakness. 'Faithless Israel' (yiśrāʾēl mĕšûbāh, apostate/turned-away Israel) is the Northern Kingdom personified as a woman — the feminine personification of national entities was common throughout ANE literature (Babylon, Tyre, Jerusalem: Ezek 16; 23; Isa 23; Rev 17-18).
7: 'I said, after she had done all this, “Return to me,” but she did not return. Then her unfaithful sister Judah saw it' — YHWH's call to return (šûbî) was extended to the Northern Kingdom before its fall — a historical reference to the prophetic mission of Hosea, Amos, and others. The failure to return triggered the Assyrian exile. But this very event was witnessed by the Southern Kingdom, Judah — 'her unfaithful sister' (ʾăḥôtāh bôgĕdāh yĕhûdāh) — which should have learned from the northern catastrophe. The sisters motif is developed at length in Ezekiel 23 (Oholah and Oholibah).
8: 'I saw that because faithless Israel had committed adultery, I had sent her away and given her a certificate of divorce; yet her unfaithful sister Judah had no fear, but went and became a prostitute too' — YHWH explicitly describes the Assyrian exile as a 'certificate of divorce' given to Israel — applying the Deuteronomy 24 framework to historical events. The theological move is significant: the exile is not raw power politics but a covenant legal action with a document to prove it. Judah's failure to fear the same consequence represents a willful disregard of the legal-historical precedent already set before her eyes.
9: 'She defiled the land by the brazen ease of her prostitution, committing adultery with stones and wooden posts' — stones (ʾāben) and wooden posts (ʿēṣ) are the standing stones (maṣṣēbôt) and Asherah poles that populated every Canaanite high place. The 'brazen ease' (miqqōl zĕnûtāh, from the sound/lightness of her prostitution) suggests that Judah's cult syncretism was casual, routine, unremarkable — not even experienced as transgression. Josiah's reform (2 Kings 23:4-15) involved specifically the physical removal of these objects from the high places, the temple, and the surrounding territory.
10: 'In spite of all this, Judah, her unfaithful sister, did not return to me with all her heart — only in pretense, declares the LORD' — the critique of Josiah's reform as 'pretense' (bišeqer, in falsehood/deception) is a striking assessment of one of the most comprehensive covenant-renewal efforts in Judah's history. The external reform — destroying high places, reinstituting Passover, reading the Torah — was real and significant; but it did not penetrate to the popular heart. Josiah's reform was royal and top-down; the popular religion reasserted itself immediately after his death (610-609 BCE), as evidenced by the rapid apostasy under Jehoiakim.
11: 'The LORD said to me: Faithless Israel has shown herself more in the right than unfaithful Judah' — the comparative judgment is shocking: the Northern Kingdom, which received the judgment of exile for its sins, is declared more righteous than Judah, which had the precedent of Israel's punishment before it and sinned anyway. Sin with full historical warning is more culpable than sin without it. This is the logic Jesus applies in Luke 12:47-48 ('the servant who knew the master's will and did not act accordingly will be beaten with many blows') — greater knowledge creates greater accountability.
12: 'Go and proclaim this to the north: “Return, O faithless Israel,” declares the LORD; “I will not look on you in anger, for I am faithful in love”' — the call to northern Israel to return represents a remarkable extension of the covenant offer beyond the political boundary of the Assyrian-held territory. 'Faithful in love' (ḥāsîd ʾānî) — YHWH identifies himself by his covenant ḥesed, the same quality he recalled in Israel's early faithfulness (Jeremiah 2:2: ḥesed nĕʿûrayik). The restoration offer is grounded in YHWH's own character, not in Israel's improved behavior. This promise seeds the eschatological northern-kingdom-return of Jeremiah 3:18 and Ezekiel 37:15-22 (the two sticks).
13: 'Only acknowledge your guilt — that you have rebelled against the LORD your God and given yourself to foreign lovers under every spreading tree' — the single condition for the return offer is raq dĕʿî ʾawōnēk — only acknowledge/know your iniquity. Not 'perform these rituals' or 'complete this penance' but simple honest acknowledgment of the covenant violation. The spreading-tree mention points to the Asherah-cult location again (cf. Jeremiah 2:20; Jeremiah 3:6). The condition of confession before restoration is the same structure as 1 John 1:9 ('If we confess our sins, he is faithful and just to forgive us').
14: 'Return, faithless children, declares the LORD, for I am your husband' — baʿaltî bākem: 'I have become master/husband over you' — the baʿal term for husband is deliberately chosen. Israel has been running after the Baals; YHWH claims to be the true baʿal (husband) they are deserting for his name's counterfeit. The one-from-a-city and two-from-a-family gathering to Zion is a picture of the eschatological remnant ingathering — small numbers surviving the judgment who will become the seed of restoration (cf. Isaiah 1:9; Romans 9:29).
15: 'I will give you shepherds after my own heart, who will lead you with knowledge and wisdom' — the promised shepherds (rōʿîm) contrast with the failed kings-as-shepherds of Israel's history. The phrase 'after my own heart' (kĕlibbî) echoes the description of David (1 Samuel 13:14; Acts 13:22) — the ideal covenant king. The promise anticipates the messianic shepherd of Jeremiah 23:5-6 ('a righteous branch' of David), Ezekiel 34:23-24 (David as the one shepherd), and Jesus's 'I am the good shepherd' (John 10:11-14).
16: 'When you have become numerous and multiplied in the land in those days ... no one will say any longer, “The ark of the covenant of the LORD.” It will not come to mind' — the promise that the ark will be forgotten in the restoration era is one of the most striking eschatological statements in the OT. The ark (ʾărôn bĕrît-Yhwh) was the central covenant symbol of the First Temple; its predicted redundancy does not mean the covenant dissolves but that the new covenant relationship will so completely internalize what the ark represented that no external symbol will be needed. This anticipates the 'no temple needed' vision of Revelation 21:22 ('I did not see a temple in the city, because the Lord God Almighty and the Lamb are its temple').
17: 'At that time Jerusalem will be called the throne of the LORD, and all nations will gather to it, to honor the name of the LORD in Jerusalem' — the eschatological Jerusalem as the throne of YHWH (replacing the ark as the locus of the divine presence) and as the gathering-point for all nations is one of the major prophetic themes (Isaiah 2:2-4; Jeremiah 60:1-5; Micah 4:1-3; Zechariah 14:16-17). The phrase 'no longer follow the stubborn inclination of their evil hearts' (šĕrîrût libbām hārāʿ) echoes Deuteronomy's consistent diagnosis of Israel's problem (Deuteronomy 29:18; Jeremiah 31:21) — the eschatological gathering reverses the internal obstruction that covenant violation demonstrated.
18: 'In those days the house of Judah will join the house of Israel, and together they will come from the northern land to the land I gave your ancestors' — the Judah-Israel reunion is one of the consistent promises of the restoration oracles: Ezekiel 37:15-22 (the two sticks joined), Hosea 1:11, Isaiah 11:12-13. The political division of the kingdom (931 BCE under Rehoboam: 1 Kings 12) is treated as a reversible consequence of covenant failure, not a permanent ethnic-tribal reality. 'From the northern land' is the Assyrian/Babylonian exile direction — both kingdoms will return from the northeast.
19: 'I had thought, “How gladly I would treat you as my children and give you a desirable land, the most beautiful inheritance among the nations”' — YHWH's lament reflects genuine divine pathos: the conditional is retrospective, describing an unfulfilled plan. The 'most beautiful inheritance among the nations' (naḥălat ṣĕbî ṣĕbāʾôt gôyim) is a high designation for the land — 'the glory of all nations' (cf. Ezekiel 20:6,15). The divine expectation ('I had thought you would call me “My Father”') is disappointed — the father-child relationship is a covenant category that Israel's behavior has denied while the covenant formally maintained it.
20: 'But like a woman who is unfaithful to her husband, you have been unfaithful to me, O house of Israel, declares the LORD' — the marriage metaphor for the covenant is most fully developed in Hosea 2, Ezekiel 16, and Ezekiel 23, but Jeremiah's deployment here is similarly devastating: the normal social category of infidelity (bāgadāh, acting treacherously) is applied to the cosmic relationship. The feminine address ('O house of Israel') maintains the personification of the nation as wife across both kingdoms — both the formally divorced northern wife and the more culpable southern wife are addressed together.
21: 'A cry is heard on the bare hilltops — the weeping and pleading of the children of Israel — because they have perverted their ways and forgotten the LORD their God' — the sound from the high places is now weeping rather than the orgiastic cult-noise of Baal worship. Whether this is a prophetic anticipation of exile-lamentation or a liturgical response within the oracle structure is debated. The parallelism is deliberate: the high places were where Israel sinned (Jeremiah 2:20; Jeremiah 3:2,6); the high places are now the sites of their mourning. The lamentation motif connects to the penitential response that follows in Jeremiah 3:22-25.
22: 'Return, faithless children, and I will heal your faithlessness. “Here we are — we come to you, for you are the LORD our God”' — the call-and-response structure: YHWH's invitation to return is immediately answered by a congregational affirmation. Whether this is a liturgical formula (like a priestly call-and-response in a covenant renewal ceremony) or a prophetic ideal response is unclear, but its placement here suggests Jeremiah is modeling the correct penitential answer to YHWH's invitation. 'I will heal your faithlessness' (ʾerpāʾ mĕšûbōtêkem) — YHWH's healing of the apostasy-wound is more than forgiveness; it is therapeutic restoration of the capacity for covenant loyalty.
23: 'Surely the commotion on the hills and mountains is a delusion; surely in the LORD our God is the salvation of Israel' — the penitential confession explicitly repudiates the high-place worship. The 'commotion' (šāwĕʾ, noise/emptiness) on the hills is the Baal cult activity that was supposed to produce rain, fertility, and security but is now acknowledged as deceptive. 'Surely in the LORD our God is the salvation of Israel' — the confession of exclusive reliance on YHWH is the covenant-loyalty declaration that the apostasy had denied.
24: 'For the shameful thing has consumed from our youth what our fathers worked for — their flocks and herds, their sons and daughters' — the 'shameful thing' (bōšet) is a polemical term for Baal, found in compound names like Ish-bosheth (man of shame, where Baal has been replaced with Bosheth: 2 Samuel 2:8; cf. 1 Chronicles 8:33 'Eshbaal'). Hosea 9:10 uses the same substitution. The confession acknowledges that Baal worship has literally consumed the agricultural and human resources of the nation — the flocks and herds promised to covenant faithfulness (Deuteronomy 28:4) have been destroyed by the very cult that was supposed to produce fertility.
25: 'Let us lie down in our shame, for our dishonor covers us; for we have sinned against the LORD our God, both we and our fathers, from our youth until today, and we have not obeyed the voice of the LORD our God' — the penitential confession is comprehensive: personal ('we have sinned'), historical ('both we and our fathers'), and temporal ('from our youth until today'). Lying down in shame is the posture of total humiliation — the opposite of the lifted head that YHWH's blessing was supposed to produce (Deuteronomy 28:13: YHWH will make you the head, not the tail). The phrase 'not obeyed the voice of the LORD our God' is the summary of covenant violation that runs through Deuteronomy and the DH (Deuteronomy 28:15,45; Jeremiah 7:28; Jeremiah 42:21; Jeremiah 44:23).
